title: How Far Can Wealth Effects Go?
gist: Paywall teaser: Tan Kai Xian argues US consumption since January 2024 has been driven by household wealth effects rather than income — disposable income growth slowed from 6.8% to 2.6% y/y while consumption rose from 5.0% to 5.8%, funded by the savings rate falling from 6.4% to 2.6%. The titular question of how far this can run is left to the paywalled body. Light card — intro only.

insights.claim: US disposable personal income growth slowed from 6.8% year-on-year in January 2024 to 2.6% in April 2026, yet consumption growth rose from 5.0% to 5.8% over the same period.
insights.speaker: Tan Kai Xian
insights.so_what: Consumption outpacing income growth is a late-cycle warning — spending is not being funded by earnings.
insights.quote: US disposable personal income growth slowed from 6.8% year-on-year in January 2024 to 2.6% in April 2026. Over the same period, consumption growth rose modestly from 5.0% to 5.8%

insights.claim: The US personal savings rate fell from 6.4% to 2.6% between January 2024 and April 2026 as consumers spent a larger share of income, with household wealth effects doing the heavy lifting for consumption.
insights.speaker: Tan Kai Xian
insights.so_what: A savings rate this low leaves consumption fragile to any drop in asset prices — watch equity/housing wealth as the swing factor.
insights.quote: as the personal savings rate fell from 6.4% to 2.6%

insights.claim: The state of the US job market and household wealth effects are the two main drivers of US consumption, and since January 2024 wealth effects have done the heavy lifting.
insights.speaker: Tan Kai Xian
insights.so_what: Reframes the consumer-resilience debate around asset prices rather than payrolls — the question is how far wealth effects can sustain spending.
insights.quote: The state of the US job market and household wealth effects are the main drivers of US consumption. Since January 2024, the latter has done the heavy lifting.
